title: Cat Refusing to Eat Wet Food for 24 Hours: Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat skipping meals? Learn why a 24-hour fast is a medium urgency concern and how to triage your cat's health at home.
content_html:
Understanding Inappetence in Cats

When a cat stops eating their preferred wet food for 24 hours, it is medically known as inappetence or anorexia. Because cats have a unique metabolism, even a short period without calories can lead to secondary health complications. It is important to monitor whether they are refusing all food or just a specific texture, as this helps differentiate between pickiness and a medical issue.

Urgency Level: Medium

A cat refusing food for 24 hours is classified as a medium urgency situation. While it may not require an emergency room visit tonight, you should contact your veterinarian for an appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours. If the lack of appetite is accompanied by vomiting, diarrhea, extreme lethargy, or yellowing of the eyes, the urgency increases to high.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a photo or video to your veterinary team can be incredibly helpful for remote triage. A clear photo of your cat's face and mouth can reveal swelling or dental issues. Additionally, a close-up of their gums (which should be bubblegum pink) can help the vet check for anemia or jaundice, while a video of their posture can indicate if they are in pain.

faqs.question: Can I wait another day to see if they eat?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to wait beyond 24 hours. Cats are prone to Hepatic Lipidosis (Fatty Liver Disease) when they stop eating, which can become life-threatening very quickly.

faqs.question: Should I try offering different foods?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can try offering a small amount of plain boiled chicken or a different flavor of wet food to see if they have an appetite, but if they refuse all options, seek veterinary advice.

faqs.question: What are common causes for sudden food refusal?
faqs.answer: Common causes include dental pain, nausea from kidney or liver issues, respiratory infections that block their sense of smell, or stress-related anxiety.
